celebration: Leaf #11 — final blends II (-nk, -ft, -lt, -lp, -lk) COMPLETES the blend branch. Home of the final-/l/ trap — the single most-missed English sound in the EAL room — met head-on with dedicated drill.

songs.title: Pink-Pink-Pink!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Pink! Pink! Pink-pink-pink!
(nk… nk… nk…)

[Hook]
Two sounds at the END — stuck together!
/n/ and /k/ — nk! nk!
Pink-pink-pink! Pink-pink-pink!
PINK! (think pink!) PINK!

[Verse 2]
Soft-soft-soft! /f/ and /t/ — ft!
Feel the end — ft! ft!
Soft-soft-soft! SOFT!
So SOFT! (shh, soft!) SOFT!

[Verse 3]
Belt-belt-belt! /l/ and /t/ — lt!
The /l/ at the end — hear it! (lllt!)
Belt-belt-belt! BELT!
Milk! Silk! (LLL — feel your tongue!)

[Verse 4]
Help-help-help! /l/ and /p/ — lp!
The /l/ at the end — hear it! (lllp!)
Help-help-help! HELP!
Gulp! Yelp! (LLL — tongue goes up!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! No end blend!
(nk… nk… nk…)

[Outro]
Pink! Soft! Belt! Help!
The /l/ at the end is REAL!
Milk — not 'miuw'! (lllk!)
Pink-pink-pink!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The home of the ~97% error trap: the final /l/. Mandarin-L1 children vocalise a syllable-final /l/ into a /w/ or /o/ ('miuw' for milk, 'bew' for bell), so verses 3 and 4 isolate the tongue-tip /l/ before the -t/-p/-k and DRILL it ('lllt', 'lllp', 'lllk' — tongue tip up behind the top teeth). The whole-word stutter forces the full ending as in Week 36. No schwa; the cluster lands crisp. Never sing letter names.

songs.title: Help the Skunk!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
Help the skunk!
Help the skunk!
The skunk is stuck! (help! help!)
Help the skunk!

[Verse 2]
Chick can help! (help! help!)
Snake can help the skunk!
Help and help and help!
Help the skunk!

[Verse 3]
Sheep can lift the skunk!
Up! Up! Help the skunk!
Help! Help! (lift! lift!)
Help the skunk!

[Outro]
The skunk… is up! (hush…)
A potato?! (POTATO!)
Help a potato?! (help! help!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'Help the skunk!' — a rescue chant. Every core word (help; pink/soft/belt land in the book and cards) plus 'lift' and 'stuck'. Punch the final /p/ on 'help' every time — the whole point of the week. Whisper the outro, potato hits loud, and of course it needs rescuing too.

spreads.artNote: A small round black-and-white skunk in the spotlight, big worried eyes, fluffy striped tail curled anxiously. Endearing, not smelly-scary.
The skunk wedged into the hollow of a log, only its striped tail and worried face poking out, tiny arms flailing. The predicament.
Chick, Sheep and Snake all rushing into frame from three directions toward the stuck skunk, urgency lines, a shared 'to the rescue' energy.
Chick tugging at the skunk's tail with both wings, feet braced, beak gritted with effort. Barely budging it.
Snake looping around the log for extra grip, adding its pull to Chick's, amber eyes squeezed with effort. Teamwork building.
Sheep getting under the log and heaving it up on its woolly back, the whole thing tilting, the skunk starting to slide free. The big lift.
The freed skunk pops out and everyone tumbles into a soft, grateful heap; the skunk's fur revealed as wonderfully soft and fluffy, being gently patted. Relief.
As a thank-you the skunk is given a bright pink belt, which it wears proudly around its middle, striking a little pose. The cast applauds.
Now a single potato is wedged into the very same log hollow, tail-less and helpless, exactly where the skunk was. The whole exhausted cast stares. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it.

extras: PATTERN TREE: leaf #11 — completes the blend branch (s-, l-, r-, final I, final II). Read pink/soft/belt/help aloud and hang it.

🚨 THE FINAL-/l/ TRAP (the ~97% error): a syllable-final /l/ becomes a /w/ or /o/ for Mandarin-L1 children — 'miuw' for milk, 'bew' for bell, 'hewp' for help. Dedicated minimal-pair cards this week: bell/bew, milk/'miuw', belt/'bewt', help/'hewp'. The fix is physical — tongue tip UP behind the top teeth for a real /l/. Drill 'lll' alone, then 'lll-t', 'lll-p', 'lll-k'.
-lp and -lk are the newly authored endings (help, gulp, yelp, milk, silk, sulk); pair them with -nk, -ft, -lt.
WHOLE-WORD echo, double repetition, as in Week 36 — the ending must fully land. Moveable alphabet: snap the ending cluster on last (pi + nk, he + lp).

teacherFocus: The second hard EAL week and the last of the blends. -nk, -ft, -lt, -lp, -lk stack two consonants at the end — the same coda-cluster challenge as Week 36 — but the star problem here is the FINAL /l/, the single most-missed sound in the English-as-additional-language classroom (~97% error). Mandarin-L1 children turn a syllable-final /l/ into a /w/ or /o/: 'miuw' for milk, 'bew' for bell, 'hewp' for help. Meet it with dedicated minimal-pair cards and a physical fix — tongue tip UP behind the top teeth for a true /l/; drill 'lll' alone, then 'lll-t', 'lll-p', 'lll-k'. Keep the whole-word echo double-length. Hang leaf #11 and celebrate: the entire blend branch of the Pattern Tree is now in leaf, and next week the magic begins.
